docs.title: ORM::ActiveRecord
docs.text:
The latest version of this documentation lives at https://gdonald.github.io/ORM-ActiveRecord/.

The homepage for ORM::ActiveRecord is https://github.com/gdonald/ORM-ActiveRecord.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
ORM::ActiveRecord is an object-relational mapping module for Raku that mostly follows the Active Record Pattern.

It supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite through a common adapter layer. Application code is the same regardless of the backend; pick one with DATABASE_URL or config/application.json. See Adapters.

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
use User;
use Page;

my User $user = User.create({fname => 'Greg'});
my Page $page = Page.create({:$user, name => 'Rakuist'});

say $user.pages.first.name;
say $page.user.fname;

my User $alfred = User.create({fname => 'Alfred'});
$page.update({user => $alfred});

say $page.user.fname

Output:

Rakuist
Greg
Alfred

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
ORM::ActiveRecord can be installed using the zef module installation tool:

zef install --/test ORM::ActiveRecord

--/test is suggested because you probably don't have a test database setup. You can of course setup a test database.

docs.title: Adapters
docs.text: ORM::ActiveRecord supports three database backends out of the box: PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite. Tests run against all three on every CI build, and the adapter API is designed so application code is the same regardless of the backend.

docs.title: Selecting an adapter
docs.text:
Two mechanisms drive adapter selection. They're checked in this order:

  1. DATABASE_URL environment variable (preferred)
  2. config/application.json (fallback)

docs.title: DATABASE_URL
docs.text:
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@host:5432/dbname?schema=public
DATABASE_URL=mysql://root:secret@127.0.0.1:3306/dbname
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:db/test.sqlite3
DATABASE_URL=sqlite::memory:

Recognised schemes:

Scheme(s) Adapter pg, postgres, postgresql PostgreSQL mysql, mysql2, mariadb MySQL sqlite, sqlite3 SQLite

Query-string parameters are passed through as adapter options (?schema=public, ?sslmode=require, etc.).

docs.title: config/application.json
docs.text:
If DATABASE_URL is unset, ORM::ActiveRecord reads config/application.json. Three example templates live in config/:

cp config/application.json-pg-example     config/application.json
cp config/application.json-mysql-example  config/application.json
cp config/application.json-sqlite-example config/application.json

The shape:

{
  "db": {
    "adapter": "pg",
    "host": "localhost",
    "port": 5432,
    "name": "ar_test",
    "user": "postgres",
    "password": "",
    "schema": "public"
  }
}

adapter must be one of pg / mysql / sqlite (the same aliases as DATABASE_URL schemes are accepted). MySQL has no separate schema concept — the name field IS the schema. SQLite only needs name (the file path) or ":memory:".

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text: The default adapter. Uses $N bind placeholders and emits INSERT … RETURNING id for surrogate-key reads. Schema introspection goes through information_schema. Boolean and timestamp values round-trip directly.

docs.title: MySQL
docs.text:
Uses ? bind placeholders and LAST_INSERT_ID() for surrogate-key reads (MySQL has no RETURNING). Booleans are stored as TINYINT(1). Identifiers are quoted with backticks.

Two MySQL-specific behaviours to be aware of:

  • Timestamps use DATETIME(6). Plain DATETIME truncates fractional seconds, which would cause created_at drift on round-trip. Microsecond precision avoids it.
  • DATETIME columns are written and read in the local timezone. DBDish::mysql parses DATETIME values with :timezone($*TZ), so coerce-write emits local-TZ strings to keep round-trips symmetric.

docs.title: libmysqlclient discovery
docs.text:
DBDish::mysql searches for libmysqlclient versions 16..21 by default. Recent installs ship version 24+, which falls outside that range. ORM::ActiveRecord's MySqlAdapter.connect works around this by setting DBIISH_MYSQL_LIB automatically, checking common Homebrew/apt paths.

If it still can't find the library, set DBIISH_MYSQL_LIB yourself:

# macOS / Homebrew
export DBIISH_MYSQL_LIB=$(brew --prefix mysql-client)/lib/libmysqlclient.dylib

# Debian/Ubuntu
export DBIISH_MYSQL_LIB=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libmysqlclient.so

docs.title: SQLite
docs.text:
Uses ? bind placeholders. Surrogate keys come from INSERT … RETURNING id on SQLite ≥ 3.35; older versions fall back to last_insert_rowid(). Schema introspection goes through pragma_table_info. Booleans are stored as INTEGER 0/1 and dates/datetimes as ISO TEXT.

SQLite requires LIMIT whenever OFFSET is used, so the adapter emits LIMIT -1 OFFSET N when an offset is set without a limit. (-1 means unbounded in SQLite.)

The database field accepts a file path (created on first connect) or the literal ":memory:" for an ephemeral in-process database — useful in tests.

docs.title: Migrations
docs.text:
ORM::ActiveRecord includes commands to migrate your database. Migrations include adding and removing tables as well as adding and removing columns and indexes.

Migration files should contain two methods: an up and a down. The up method is the forward change you want to perform. The down method should contain what you want to happen if you decide to rollback the changes from the up method.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
db/migrate/001-create-users.raku

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Schema::Migration;

class CreateUsers is Migration {
  method up {
    self.create-table: 'users', [
      fname => { :string, limit => 32 },
      lname => { :string, limit => 32 }
    ]
  }

  method down {
    self.drop-table: 'users';
  }
}

db/migrate/002-create-pages.raku

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Schema::Migration;

class CreatePages is Migration {
  method up {
    self.create-table: 'pages', [
      user => { :reference },
      name => { :string, limit => 32 }
    ]
  }

  method down {
    self.drop-table: 'pages';
  }
}

docs.title: Timestamps
docs.text:
Most tables benefit from created_at and updated_at columns. ORM::ActiveRecord adds them with add-timestamps and manages them automatically: created_at is set on insert, updated_at is set on every save.

The exact column type is adapter-aware:

Adapter Generated DDL PostgreSQL TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now() MySQL DATETIME(6) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(6) (microsecond precision) SQLite DATETIME NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP 
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Schema::Migration;

class CreateArticles is Migration {
  method up {
    self.create-table: 'articles', [
      title => { :string, limit => 64 },
      body  => { :text },
    ];
    self.add-timestamps: 'articles';
  }

  method down {
    self.drop-table: 'articles';
  }
}

You can also declare datetime columns explicitly with :datetime (or :timestamp):

self.add-column: 'articles', :published_at => { :datetime }

To remove the timestamp columns:

self.remove-timestamps: 'articles';

docs.title: Run Migrations
docs.text:
New migrations can be ran using the provided ar command. It its most simple form ar will run all outstanding up methods.

$ ar

Other migration options include the ability to only migrate up or down a specific number of migrations:

$ ar up      # runs all pending migrations
$ ar down    # resets all migrations, be careful!
$ ar up:1    # runs 1 pending migrations
$ ar down:2  # resets 2 previously completed migrations

docs.title: Tests
docs.text: ORM::ActiveRecord includes a full test suite. It runs against PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite. ./test.raku cycles through all three; any adapter that isn't reachable is skipped with a message describing how to enable it.

docs.title: Database configuration
docs.text:
There are two ways to point the suite at a database:

1. DATABASE_URL env var (preferred — what ./test.raku and CI use):

DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/ar_test?schema=public
DATABASE_URL=mysql://root:root@127.0.0.1:3306/ar_test
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:db/test.sqlite3

For local multi-adapter runs, ./test.raku reads per-adapter overrides from AR_PG_URL, AR_MYSQL_URL, and AR_SQLITE_URL. The PostgreSQL default is built from config/application.json if present.

2. config/application.json (single-adapter; what bin/ar reads when DATABASE_URL is unset). Pick the example matching your adapter:

cp config/application.json-pg-example     config/application.json
cp config/application.json-mysql-example  config/application.json
cp config/application.json-sqlite-example config/application.json

Then edit credentials as needed.

docs.title: Running the suite
docs.text:
./test.raku is the canonical entry point. With no DATABASE_URL set, it cycles through PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite — probing each first and skipping unreachable adapters with a message describing how to enable them. With DATABASE_URL set, it runs once against that adapter (this is what CI does per matrix entry).

$ ./test.raku

When all three adapters are reachable you get a runtimes summary at the end:

==> Runtimes
  postgres    26.60s
  mysql       26.37s
  sqlite      22.59s
  total       75.65s

docs.title: Running with prove6
docs.text:
To run the suite once against your default adapter — what config/application.json or DATABASE_URL points at — invoke prove6 directly:

$ prove6 -Ilib t

docs.title: Running a single test file
docs.text:
$ raku -Ilib t/0020-basic.rakutest

Test files live under t/ and use the .rakutest extension. They follow the naming pattern NNNN-name.rakutest where NNNN is a sort key, not a stable identifier — feel free to renumber when reorganising.

docs.title: Aggregation and Selection
docs.text: Beyond filtering, relations expose set-shaping operations: deduplication, grouping, subquery sources, and per-relation flags.

docs.title: distinct
docs.text:
distinct adds SELECT DISTINCT. Applies across all selected columns.

User.distinct.count;                        # = total user count
User.select('lname').distinct.pluck('lname');
User.select('lname').distinct.count;        # = number of unique lnames

distinct(False) clears the flag again. unscope(:distinct) does the same.

User.select('lname').distinct.distinct(False).pluck('lname');
User.distinct.unscope(:distinct);

docs.title: group and having
docs.text:
group(*@cols) adds GROUP BY. having($predicate, *@binds) adds a HAVING clause; binds are parameterised.

User.group('lname').count;                                    # 1 row per lname
User.group('lname').having('count(*) > 1').pluck('lname');
User.group('lname').having('count(*) > ?', 1).count;

regroup(*@cols) replaces any prior group clause. unscope(:group) and unscope(:having) drop the respective clause.

User.group('lname').regroup('fname');           # only fname grouping remains
User.group('lname').unscope(:group).all;        # plain SELECT

docs.title: from
docs.text:
from($source [, $alias]) replaces the implicit FROM table_name. Use it for subqueries or aliased base tables.

User.from('users').count;
User.from('(SELECT * FROM users WHERE lname != ?) users', 'Brown').all;

my $q = User.from('users AS u', 'u');
$q.from-alias;                                    # 'u'
$q.from-source;                                   # 'users AS u'

unscope(:from) resets to the default table.

docs.title: readonly
docs.text:
A readonly relation produces records that refuse to save / update / destroy. Useful when a query crosses join tables and the records should not be persisted back.

my @users    = User.readonly.all;          # @users[0].save throws
my @writable = User.readonly.unscope(:readonly).all;

merge propagates the flag:

User.all.merge(User.readonly).readonly-value;     # True

docs.title: extending
docs.text:
extending(*@modules) mixes additional methods into the relation. Useful for paginators or custom finders local to a query chain.

role Pagination {
  method page(Int $n, Int :$per = 25) {
    self.limit($per).offset(($n - 1) * $per);
  }
}

my @page1 = User.order('id').extending(Pagination).page(1).all;
my @page2 = User.order('id').extending(Pagination).page(2).all;

You can pass multiple modules; their methods compose in declaration order.

role NameOnly {
  method names { self.pluck('fname') }
}

User.order('id').extending(Pagination, NameOnly).page(1).names;

docs.title: Model Callbacks
docs.text:
ORM::ActiveRecord supports callbacks that can be performed during various life cycle events.

These callbacks currently include after-create, after-save, after-update, after-destroy, before-create, before-save, before-update, and before-destroy.

docs.title: After Create
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Log is Model {};

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence };
    self.after-create: -> { self.log };
  }

  method log {
    my $log = self.email ~ ' was created';
    Log.create({:$log});
  }
}

# No logs to start with
say Log.count == 0;

# Creating a record creates a log
my $client = Client.create({ email => 'fred@aol.com' });
say Log.count == 1;

# Updating a record does not create a log
$client.email = 'barney@compuserve.net';
$client.save;
say Log.count == 1;

Output

True
True
True

docs.title: After Save
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Log is Model {};

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence };
    self.after-save: -> { self.log };
  }

  method log {
    my $log = self.email ~ ' was saved';
    Log.create({:$log});
  }
}

# No logs to start with
say Log.count == 0;

# Creating a record creates a log
my $client = Client.create({ email => 'fred@aol.com' });
say Log.count == 1;

# Updating a record also creates a log
$client.email = 'barney@compuserve.net';
$client.save;
say Log.count == 2;

Output

True
True
True

docs.title: After Update
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Log is Model {};

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence };
    self.after-update: -> { self.log };
  }

  method log {
    my $log = self.email ~ ' was updated';
    Log.create({:$log});
  }
}

# No logs to start with
say Log.count == 0;

# Creating a record does not create a log
my $client = Client.create({ email => 'fred@aol.com' });
say Log.count == 0;

# Updating a record creates a log
$client.email = 'barney@compuserve.net';
$client.save;
say Log.count == 1;

Output

True
True
True

docs.title: Before Create
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence };
    self.before-create: -> { self.lowercase-email };
  }

  method lowercase-email {
    self.email .= lc;
  }
}

# Email is lower-cased before the record is created
my $client = Client.create({ email => 'Fred@AOL.com' });
say $client.email eq 'fred@aol.com';

# Email is not lower-cased before the record is updated
$client.email = 'BARNEY@compuserve.NET';
$client.save;
say $client.email eq 'BARNEY@compuserve.NET';

Output

True
True

docs.title: Before Save
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence };
    self.before-save: -> { self.lowercase-email };
  }

  method lowercase-email {
    self.email .= lc;
  }
}

# Email is lower-cased before the record is created
my $client = Client.create({ email => 'Fred@AOL.com' });
say $client.email eq 'fred@aol.com';

# Email is also lower-cased before the record is updated
$client.email = 'BARNEY@compuserve.NET';
$client.save;
say $client.email eq 'barney@compuserve.net';

Output

True
True

docs.title: After Destroy
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Log is Model {};

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence };
    self.after-destroy: -> { self.log };
  }

  method log {
    my $log = self.email ~ ' was destroyed';
    Log.create({:$log});
  }
}

my $client = Client.create({ email => 'fred@aol.com' });
say Log.count == 0;

$client.destroy;
say Log.count == 1;

Output

True
True

docs.title: Before Destroy
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.before-destroy: -> { say 'about to destroy ' ~ self.email };
  }
}

my $client = Client.create({ email => 'fred@aol.com' });
$client.destroy;

Output

about to destroy fred@aol.com

If you want to remove a record without firing destroy callbacks (or any other side effects), use delete instead. delete issues the DELETE directly and skips the before-destroy and after-destroy callbacks.

docs.title: Before Update
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence };
    self.before-update: -> { self.lowercase-email };
  }

  method lowercase-email {
    self.email .= lc;
  }
}

# Email is not lower-cased before the record is created
my $client = Client.create({ email => 'Fred@AOL.com' });
say $client.email eq 'Fred@AOL.com';

# Email is lower-cased before the record is saved
$client.save;
say $client.email eq 'fred@aol.com';

Output

True
True

docs.title: Finders
docs.text: ORM::ActiveRecord provides a range of class-level finder methods for locating records.

docs.title: find
docs.text:
find($id) looks up a record by primary key. It raises X::RecordNotFound if no row matches.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::X;

my $user = User.find(42);

try {
  User.find(99_999);

  CATCH {
    when X::RecordNotFound {
      say .message;   # Couldn't find User with id=99999
    }
  }
}

docs.title: find-by
docs.text:
find-by(%conditions) returns the first matching record, or Nil if there is no match.

my $user = User.find-by({fname => 'Greg'});
if $user.defined {
  say $user.lname;
}

find-by-or-die(%conditions) raises X::RecordNotFound instead of returning Nil.

my $user = User.find-by-or-die({fname => 'Greg'});

docs.title: first / last / take
docs.text:
first and last return a single record ordered by id. take returns up to N records with no order guarantee.

User.where({active => True}).first;   # ORDER BY id LIMIT 1
User.where({active => True}).last;    # ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1

User.take;        # one record (LIMIT 1)
User.take(5);     # up to five records

When you need a different order, chain .order before .first / .last:

User.order('lname').first;

docs.title: exists
docs.text:
exists returns True if any row matches.

User.exists;                       # True if the table has any rows
User.exists({fname => 'Greg'});    # True if at least one match

docs.title: Joins
docs.text: joins and left-outer-joins add SQL JOIN clauses to a relation. They accept several forms:

docs.title: By association name
docs.text:
Pass an association name (defined via has-many / belongs-to):

Subscription.joins('user').count;            # belongs_to side
User.joins(:subscriptions).count;            # has_many side
User.joins(:magazines).count;                # has_many :through

The join condition (subscriptions.user_id = users.id, etc.) is derived from the association definition.

docs.title: Nested associations
docs.text:
A nested-hash form follows multi-level associations:

User.joins(:subscriptions(:magazine)).count;

This emits a join through subscriptions then magazines.

docs.title: Raw SQL
docs.text:
For one-offs that don't map to an association, pass a literal join clause:

my $raw = 'INNER JOIN subscriptions ON subscriptions.user_id = users.id';
User.joins($raw).count;

docs.title: left-outer-joins
docs.text:
left-outer-joins keeps rows from the base table even when no related row exists. Useful for "find users with or without subscriptions".

User.left-outer-joins(:subscriptions).count;            # all users
User.left-outer-joins(:subscriptions).distinct.count;   # all users, deduped

docs.title: Filtering on a joined table
docs.text:
Once you've joined, where accepts a nested hash to filter on the joined table's columns.

Subscription.joins(:user).where({users => {fname => 'Alice'}}).all;
User.joins(:subscriptions(:magazine))
    .where({magazines => {title => 'Mad'}})
    .distinct
    .all;

A bare column name in where after a join still resolves to the base table:

User.joins(:subscriptions).where({fname => 'Alice'}).distinct.count;
# WHERE users.fname = 'Alice'

docs.title: references
docs.text:
references(*@table-names) is a hint that named tables will be filtered or ordered against. It pre-declares the join targets without altering row counts itself, which matters when combining with eager loading (where the difference between preload and eager_load depends on whether you reference the joined table elsewhere).

User.references('posts').count;
User.references('posts', 'comments');

docs.title: distinct under joins
docs.text:
A joins on a has_many returns one row per join row, so deduping with distinct is common when counting base records.

User.joins(:subscriptions).count;             # = total subscription count
User.joins(:subscriptions).distinct.count;    # = users with >= 1 subscription

docs.title: Models
docs.text: ORM::ActiveRecord supports model relationships, the layer of your app that is responsible for managing data and business logic.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
In this example the user has many pages and the reverse is also true, a page belongs to a `user.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Page {...} # forward declaration

class User is Model is export {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.has-many: pages => class => Page;
  }
}

class Page is Model is export {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.belongs-to: user => class => User;
  }
}

Perl 6 does single-pass compilation. You must provide forward declarations {...} for any models that have not been defined yet but need to be used in has-many or belongs-to relationships.

This allows chaining method calls together like this:

my $user = User.create({fname => 'Greg'});
my $page = Page.create({:$user, name => 'Rakuist'});

say $user.pages.first.name;
say $page.user.fname;

Output

Rakuist
Greg

Access to lower level foreign key relationships is supported.

say $page.user_id == $user.id;

Output

True

docs.title: Where Query
docs.text:
To search for a particular record you can issue a where query with a hash for parameters.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { :presence }
  }
}

my $fred = User.create({fname => 'Fred'});
my $barney = User.create({fname => 'Barney'});

my $result = User.where({fname => 'Fred'}).first;
say $result == $fred;

Output

True

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Frequently used queries can be made into a scope for easier access. Scopes live on the model type, not on an instance of the type, so we use $?CLASS to point to the existing class. The contents of the scope is a lazy block that is only evaluated when required.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Image is Model {
  $?CLASS.scope: 'jpgs', -> { $?CLASS.where({ext => 'jpg'}) }

  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'name', { :presence }
    self.validate: 'ext', { :presence, inclusion => { in => <jpg png> } }
  }
}

my $foo = Image.create({name => 'foo', ext => 'jpg'});
my $bar = Image.create({name => 'bar', ext => 'jpg'});
my $baz = Image.create({name => 'baz', ext => 'png'});
say Image.count;

my @images = Image.jpgs.all;
say any(@images) == $foo;
say any(@images) == $bar;
say none(@images) == $baz;

Output

3
True
True
True

docs.title: Has Many Through
docs.text:
You can access related models using has-many with through.

In this example a user has access to magazines through the subscriptions model:

class Subscription {...} # stub

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.has-many: subscriptions => class => Subscription;
    self.has-many: magazines => through => :subscriptions;
  }
}

class Magazine is Model {}

class Subscription is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.belongs-to: user => class => User;
    self.belongs-to: magazine => class => Magazine;
  }
}

my $user = User.create({fname => 'Greg'});
my $magazine = Magazine.create({title => 'Mad'});
Subscription.create({:$user, :$magazine});

say $user.magazines.first == $magazine;

Output

True

docs.title: Is Dirty
docs.text:
If you modify a record it will need to be persisted back to the database or the changes will eventually be lost. To know if you actually have pending changes that need to be saved you can call is-dirty on the model instance.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { :presence }
  }
}

my $user = User.create({fname => 'Fred'});
say $user.is-dirty;

$user.fname = 'John';
say $user.is-dirty;

$user.save;
say $user.is-dirty;

Output

False
True
False

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text: ORM::ActiveRecord exposes both quiet (return False on failure) and loud (raise an exception on failure) variants of the persistence methods. Use whichever fits the situation: forms and user-driven flows usually want the quiet variants so they can re-render with errors, while scripts, callbacks, and worker code usually want the loud variants so failures aren't silently swallowed.

docs.title: Save and Update
docs.text:
save and update return True on success or False if validation failed. Inspect errors to see what went wrong.

my $user = User.new;
$user.fname = '';

unless $user.save {
  for $user.errors.errors -> $e {
    say $e.field.name ~ ' ' ~ $e.message;
  }
}

save-or-die and update-or-die raise X::RecordInvalid instead. The exception carries the failing record and a list of human-readable messages.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::X;

try {
  User.create-or-die({fname => ''});

  CATCH {
    when X::RecordInvalid {
      say .message;        # Validation failed: fname must be present
      say .messages;       # (fname must be present)
      say .record.fname;   # ''
    }
  }
}

create-or-die is a class-level convenience that builds the record, runs validations, and raises if it could not be saved.

docs.title: Destroy and Delete
docs.text:
destroy removes the record from the database and fires the before-destroy and after-destroy callbacks. Use it when associated cleanup, logging, or notifications need to run.

class Page is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.after-destroy: -> { say "Page #{self.id} torn down" };
  }
}

my $page = Page.create({name => 'Welcome'});
$page.destroy;        # fires the after-destroy callback
say $page.id;         # 0  -- the in-memory record's id is cleared

delete issues the DELETE directly without callbacks. Use it when you want a side-effect-free removal (for example, in tests or in a destroy callback for a parent record).

$page.delete;         # no callbacks, single SQL DELETE

destroy-all is a class-level convenience that wipes every row in the table without instantiating records or firing per-row callbacks.

Page.destroy-all;

docs.title: Automatic Timestamps
docs.text:
If a table has created_at and/or updated_at columns, ORM::ActiveRecord manages them for you. created_at is set on insert; updated_at is set on every save (insert and update).

See Migrations » Timestamps for the column setup.

docs.title: Queries
docs.text: This page covers the filtering and relation-modification vocabulary that builds on the basics in Relations. Everything here returns a new relation — the original is never mutated.

docs.title: where shorthands
docs.text:
where(%conditions) accepts several value shapes:

Shape SQL emitted where({col => $value}) col = ? where({col => Nil}) col IS NULL where({col => [a, b, c]}) col IN (?, ?, ?) where({col => 1..10}) col BETWEEN ? AND ? where({assoc => $instance}) assoc_id = ? (uses $instance.id) 
User.where({age => 18..65});
User.where({email => Nil});
User.where({id => [1, 2, 3]});
User.where({user => $alice});            # belongs_to :user

docs.title: where.not
docs.text:
where.not(%conditions) adds a negated condition. Nil, ranges, and arrays work the same as the positive form.

User.where.not({fname => 'Bob'}).count;
User.where.not({email => Nil}).all;          # IS NOT NULL
User.where.not({age => 18..65}).all;         # NOT BETWEEN
User.where.not({id => [1, 2, 3]}).all;       # NOT IN (...)

docs.title: where.missing and where.associated
docs.text:
For relations through associations, where.missing(:assoc) finds records with no associated rows; where.associated(:assoc) finds those that have at least one. Both work over belongs_to, has_many, and has_many :through.

User.where.missing(:subscriptions).all;       # users with no subscription
User.where.associated(:subscriptions).all;    # users with at least one
User.where.missing(:magazines).all;           # honors :through

Model.associated(:assoc) is a class-level shortcut for the common case.

docs.title: excluding
docs.text:
excluding(*@records-or-ids) removes specific rows from the result set, mirroring WHERE id NOT IN (...).

User.excluding($bob).all;                          # by instance
User.excluding($alice, $dave).all;                 # multiple
User.excluding(1, 2, 3).all;                       # by id

It composes naturally:

User.where({lname => 'Anderson'}).excluding($alice).all;

docs.title: or and and
docs.text:
or(other_relation) and and(other_relation) combine WHERE clauses across two relations. The result is a single SQL query with the conditions wrapped in the right boolean structure.

User.where({fname => 'Alice'}).or(User.where.not({fname => 'Carol'})).all;
User.where({active => True}).and(User.where({verified => True})).all;

docs.title: merge
docs.text:
merge(other_relation) folds another relation's clauses into this one. Last-wins semantics: other takes precedence for limit, offset, distinct, and readonly. where, order, joins, and similar additive clauses are concatenated.

my $base   = User.where({active => True});
my $sorted = User.order('lname');
my @rows   = $base.merge($sorted).all;

# Override pagination from another relation
User.limit(10).merge(User.limit(2)).all;     # uses LIMIT 2

docs.title: rewhere
docs.text:
rewhere(%conditions) replaces any prior where conditions on the same columns. Useful when narrowing-then-broadening.

my $q = User.where({active => True, role => 'admin'});
$q.rewhere({role => 'superadmin'}).all;     # active=True still, role swapped

docs.title: unscope
docs.text:
unscope(:scope) surgically removes a single relation dimension:

User.where({active => True}).unscope(:where).all;   # drop all WHERE
User.order('lname').unscope(:order).all;             # drop ORDER BY
User.group('lname').unscope(:group).all;             # drop GROUP BY
User.distinct.unscope(:distinct).all;                # drop DISTINCT

You can also drop a single where condition by column:

User.where({active => True, role => 'admin'})
    .unscope(where => :role)
    .all;

docs.title: Relations
docs.text:
User.where(...), User.order(...), and friends return a chainable relation that defers running SQL until you ask for results. Relations compose: every scope-narrowing call returns a new relation, leaving the original untouched.

my $active = User.where({active => True});
my $recent = $active.order('created_at DESC').limit(10);

# $active and $recent are independent relations; neither has hit the DB yet.
my @rows = $recent.all;   # one query, fired here

docs.title: Realising a relation
docs.text:
Relations stay lazy until you call one of these methods:

Method Returns .all List of model instances .first One instance ordered by id ASC, or Nil .last One instance ordered by id DESC, or Nil .count Int — COUNT(*) .exists Bool .pluck(...) List of raw column values .ids List of id column values (= pluck('id'))

docs.title: where
docs.text:
where(%conditions) adds equality conditions joined with AND. Conditions are bound as parameters, never interpolated, so user-supplied values are safe.

User.where({active => True, fname => 'Greg'});

You can chain further .where(...) calls to merge in additional conditions.

my $q = User.where({active => True});
$q = $q.where({fname => 'Greg'}) if $only-greg;
my @users = $q.all;

where accepts several value shorthands beyond a literal scalar:

User.where({age   => 18..65});           # BETWEEN
User.where({email => Nil});              # IS NULL
User.where({id    => [1, 2, 3]});        # IN
User.where({user  => $alice});           # auto-uses $alice.id as user_id

See Queries for the full filtering vocabulary, including where.not, where.missing, where.associated, or, and, merge, rewhere, unscope, and excluding.

docs.title: order
docs.text:
order(*@cols) adds ORDER BY clauses. Pass column names or fully formed fragments like 'fname DESC'.

User.order('lname');
User.order('lname', 'fname');
User.order('created_at DESC');

reorder(...) replaces any prior order clauses. in-order-of(:col, [...]) orders rows to match an explicit value list.

User.order('lname').reorder('id');                       # only 'id' is applied
User.in-order-of(:id, [3, 1, 2]).all;                    # rows in [3, 1, 2] order

docs.title: limit and offset
docs.text:
limit(N) and offset(N) add LIMIT and OFFSET. Useful for pagination.

sub page-of-users(Int :$page = 1, Int :$per = 20) {
  User.order('id').limit($per).offset(($page - 1) * $per).all;
}

SQLite and MySQL require a LIMIT whenever an OFFSET is set; the adapter adds a synthetic unbounded LIMIT when you pass offset alone.

docs.title: all
docs.text:
Model.all returns a relation that, once realised, returns every row. You can chain conditions onto it just like where.

User.all.where({active => True}).order('lname').all;

docs.title: pluck and ids
docs.text:
pluck returns raw column values without instantiating model objects. It is much cheaper than materialising records and dropping everything but one column.

my @fnames = User.pluck('fname');
# (Alice Bob Carol Dave Eve)

my @rows = User.order('id').pluck('fname', 'lname');
# ((Alice Anderson) (Bob Brown) ...)

ids is the common shorthand for pluck('id').

my @ids = User.where({active => True}).ids;

docs.title: count and exists
docs.text:
count returns the number of matching rows. exists returns True if any row matches.

User.where({active => True}).count;
User.where({active => True}).exists;

docs.title: Validation Conditionals
docs.text: Validations can be made conditional based on some other method call or criteria. The available conditionals are if, unless, and on. With on a create or update life cycle event may be specified.

docs.title: If
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Book is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'title', { :presence, :if => { self.returns-true } }
  }

  method returns-true { True }
}

my $book = Book.build;
say $book.is-valid;
say $book.errors.title[0];

Output

False
must be present

docs.title: Unless
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Book is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'title', { :presence, :unless => { self.returns-false } }
  }

  method returns-false { False }
}

my $book = Book.build;
say $book.is-valid;
say $book.errors.title[0];

Output

False
must be present

docs.title: Create
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { :presence, on => { :create } }
  }
}

my $user = User.create({});
say $user.is-valid;
say $user.errors.fname[0];

Output

False
must be present

docs.title: Update
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { :presence, on => { :update } }
  }
}

my $user = User.create({});
say $user.is-valid;

$user.update({fname => ''});
say $user.is-valid;
say $user.errors.fname[0];

Output

True
False
must be present

docs.title: Data Validations
docs.text: ORM::ActiveRecord supports many forms of data validations, when they occur and if they occur at all.

docs.title: Acceptance
docs.text:
The acceptance validation requires a field value to be True for the Model instance to be valid.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Contract is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'terms', { :acceptance }
  }
}

A value of False will make the instance invalid and the validated field will contain an error.

my $contract = Contract.create({name => 'Offical Document', terms => False});
say $contract.is-valid;
say $contract.errors.terms[0];

Output

False
must be accepted

docs.title: Confirmation
docs.text:
The confirmation validation requires a field to be "confirmed" for the Model instance to be valid. A virtual field with a suffix of "_confirmed" is required. Combining confirmation with presence is usually helpful.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Client is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { :presence, :confirmation }
  }
}

my $client = Client.build({email => 'fred@aol.com'});
say $client.is-invalid;
say $client.errors.email[0];

$client = Client.build({email => 'fred@aol.com', email_confirmation => 'fred@aol.com'});
say $client.is-valid;

Output

False
must be confirmed
True

docs.title: Exclusion
docs.text:
The exclusion validation prevents certain values from being entered. The excluded values are defined using in.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Person is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'username', { exclusion => { in => <admin superuser> } }
  }
}

my $person = Person.build({username => 'admin'});
say $person.is-valid;
say $person.errors.username[0];

Output

False
is invalid

docs.title: Format
docs.text:
The format validation requires a field value match a specific regular expression. The format is defined using with.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Contact is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'email', { format => { with => /:i ^<[\w]>+ '@' <[\w]>+ '.' <[\w]>+$/ } }
  }
}

my $contact = Contact.create({email => 'foo'});
say $contact.is-valid;
say $contact.errors.email[0];

Output

False
is invalid

docs.title: Inclusion
docs.text:
The inclusion validation requires a field value exist in a list of pre-defined values. The allowed values are defined using in.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Image is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'format', { inclusion => { in => <gif jpeg jpg png> } }
  }
}

my $image = Image.build({format => 'foo'});
say $image.is-invalid;
say $image.errors.format[0];

Output

False
is invalid

docs.title: Length
docs.text:
The length validation requires a field value to be a certain length to be valid.

Valid values are defined using min, max, is, or in.

docs.title: Minimum
docs.text:
A min length validation requires the length of the field value be a specified minimum:

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { length => { min => 4 } }
  }
}

my $user = User.build({fname => 'Joe'});
say $user.is-valid;
say $user.errors.fname[0];

Output

False
at least 4 characters required

docs.title: Maximum
docs.text:
A max length validation requires a field value length be less than a specified value.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { length => { max => 12 } }
  }
}

my $user = User.build({fname => 'Michaelangelo'});
say $user.is-valid;
say $user.errors.fname[0];

Output

False
only 12 characters allowed

docs.title: In a range
docs.text:
An in length validation requires the field value length be within a specified range.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { length => { in => 4..32 } }
  }
}

my $user = User.build({fname => 'Joe'});
say $user.is-valid;
say $user.errors.fname[0];

Output

False
4 to 32 characters required

docs.title: Is exactly
docs.text:
An is length validation requires a value be an exact size:

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { length => { is => 8 } }
  }
}

my $user = User.build({fname => 'Joe'});
say $user.is-valid;
say $user.errors.fname[0];

Output

False
exactly 8 characters required

docs.title: Numericality
docs.text: The numericality validation requires a field value to be numerical and of a specific value or in a specific range. Values can be specified using gt, gte, lt, lte, as well as in for ranges.

docs.title: Less than
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Book is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'pages', { numericality => { lt => 400 } }
  }
}

my $book = Book.create({pages => 400});
say $book.is-valid;
say $book.errors.pages[0];

Output

False
less than 400 required

docs.title: Less than or equal
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Book is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'pages', { numericality => { lte => 400 } }
  }
}

my $book = Book.create({pages => 401});
say $book.is-valid;
say $book.errors.pages[0];

Output

False
400 or less required

docs.title: Greater than
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Book is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'pages', { numericality => { gt => 400 } }
  }
}

my $book = Book.create({pages => 400});
say $book.is-valid;
say $book.errors.pages[0];

Output

False
more than 400 required

docs.title: Greater than or equal
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Book is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'pages', { numericality => { gte => 400 } }
  }
}

my $book = Book.create({pages => 399});
say $book.is-valid;
say $book.errors.pages[0];

Output

False
400 or more required

docs.title: In a range
docs.text:
use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Book is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'pages', { numericality => { in => 400..1000 } }
  }
}

my $book = Book.create({pages => 399});
say $book.is-valid;
say $book.errors.pages[0];

Output

False
400 to 1000 required

docs.title: Presence
docs.text:
The presence validation requires a field value to simply exist for the Model instance to be valid.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'fname', { :presence }
  }
}

my $user = User.build;
say $user.is-valid;
say $user.errors.fname[0];

Output

False
must be present

docs.title: Uniqueness
docs.text:
The uniqueness validation requires a field value to be unique with respect to existing field value data in the database.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Person is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.validate: 'username', { :uniqueness }
  }
}

my $person = Person.create({username => 'alfred'});
say $person.is-valid;

my $person_2 = Person.build({username => 'alfred'});
say $person_2.is-valid;
say $person_2.errors.username[0];

Output

True
False
must be unique

docs.title: Unique Scope
docs.text:
The uniqueness validation can also contain a scope. This, for example, can be used for validating uniqueness within a specific foreign key.

use ORM::ActiveRecord::Model;

class Subscription {...}

class User is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.has-many: subscriptions => class => Subscription;   
    self.validate: 'fname', { :presence }
  }
}

class Magazine is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.has-many: subscriptions => class => Subscription;
    self.validate: 'title', { :presence }
  }
}

class Subscription is Model {
  submethod BUILD {
    self.belongs-to: user => class => User;
    self.belongs-to: magazine => class => Magazine;
    self.validate: 'user_id', { uniqueness => scope => :magazine_id }
  }
}

my $user = User.create({fname => 'Greg'});
my $magazine = Magazine.create({title => 'Mad'});
my $subscription = Subscription.create({:$user, :$magazine});
say $subscription.is-valid;

my $subscription_2 = Subscription.build({:$user, :$magazine});
say $subscription_2.is-valid;
say $subscription_2.errors.user_id[0];

Output

True
False
must be unique
